message.content:
Research Unix/Linux RC file systems and dotfile conventions.

Focus on:
1. Most sophisticated RC systems:
   - .bashrc, .zshrc (shell)
   - .npmrc, .yarnrc
   - .eslintrc, .prettierrc
   - .gitconfig
   - .editorconfig

2. For each, document:
   - File naming convention
   - Import/extend/inherit mechanisms
   - Cascade behavior (global → user → project → local)
   - File format (JSON, YAML, INI, custom)

3. Which has the most sophisticated features?
   - Imports from other files
   - Extends/inheritance
   - Override patterns
   - Environment variable expansion

4. Historical origin of "rc" (run commands)

Return a structured comparison of RC systems and their sophistication levels.

input.query: Unix RC file history origin "run commands" dotfiles convention
npmrc yarnrc configuration cascade global user project local

How to Convert & Configure for Yarn Install — w3tutorials.net","url":"https://www.w3tutorials.net/blog/confused-about-yarnrc-and-npmrc/"},{"title":"Install private packages with Yarn · Gemfury Dev Center","url":"https://gemfury.com/help/private-yarn/"},{"title":"Setting Yarn config defaults globally | Aamnah - Frontend Web Developer","url":"https://aamnah.com/notes/nodejs/set-yarn-config-global-defaults/"},{"title":"Using different registries in yarn and npm | by Crysfel Villa | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@crysfel/using-different-registries-in-yarn-and-npm-766541d6f851"},{"title":"NPM & Yarn Private Package Registry Personal Mistakes • Andrei Calazans","url":"https://andrei-calazans.com/posts/2020-08-19-private-package-registry-bug/"}]

Based on the search results, here's information about npmrc and yarnrc configuration cascade across global, user, project, and local levels:

## Configuration File Locations

### .npmrc
.npmrc can exist in two locations: Project-level (./.npmrc in your project root) which applies only to that project, and Global (~/.npmrc in user home directory) which applies to all npm commands run by the user.

### .yarnrc (Yarn 1) and .yarnrc.yml (Yarn 2+)
Yarn 1's .yarnrc is similar to .npmrc but uses slightly different prefixes for registry URLs. It supports project-level (./.yarnrc) and global (~/.yarnrc) files.(no content)The global config is saved in ~/.yarnrc.yml (Yarn 2.0 onwards, previously it was called .yarnrc). If you set a config flag in a directory where there is no .yarnrc.yml, it'll add the config to the global config file at ~/.yarnrc.yml.(no content)Yarn 1 and Yarn 2+ have distinct config structures—Yarn 1 uses .yarnrc (INI-style), and Yarn 2+ uses .yarnrc.yml (YAML with npmRegistries/npmScopes).

## Configuration Cascade Behavior

When you run yarn install it searches for .npmrc and .yarnrc from your directory all the way up to your home directory (~). You can see this happen by running yarn install --verbose.(no content)Yarn composes all of the configuration values from .npmrc and .yarnrc into a single global dictionary which you can verify by running yarn config list.(no content)The interesting part about this is the overriding behavior of the deeper configs. If you are currently in ~/projects/cool_app/, the .npmrc file in this directory will take priority over the one in ~.

## Yarn Modern (v2+) Differences

Yarn modern does not recognize --registry or .npmrc file anymore. For defining a registry you must use the .yarnrc.yml located in the root of your project or global configuration.

## Project-specific Configuration

If your project relies on private packages, it's recommended to commit a project-specific configuration to ensure consistent installs. You can commit a .yarnrc file to the root directory of your project.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "editorconfig inheritance override patterns specification"

Links: [{"title":"EditorConfig","url":"https://editorconfig.org/"},{"title":"Configuration files for code analysis rules - .NET | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/fundamentals/code-analysis/configuration-files"},{"title":".NET code style rule options - .NET | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/fundamentals/code-analysis/code-style-rule-options"},{"title":"Apply global config and override for certain files · Issue #220 · editorconfig/editorconfig","url":"https://github.com/editorconfig/editorconfig/issues/220"},{"title":"Reasons to use both a local and global editorconfig file - Daniel Schroeder’s Programming Blog","url":"https://blog.danskingdom.com/Reasons-to-use-both-a-local-and-global-editorconfig-file/"},{"title":"The .editorconfig files for .NET developers - NDepend Blog","url":"https://blog.ndepend.com/the-editorconfig-files-for-net-developers/"},{"title":"Define consistent coding styles with EditorConfig - Visual Studio (Windows) | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/visualstudio/ide/create-portable-custom-editor-options?view=vs-2022"},{"title":"Use EditorConfig | JetBrains Rider Documentation","url":"https://www.jetbrains.com/help/rider/Using_EditorConfig.html"},{"title":"Working with EditorConfig in Visual Studio 2019 | Azure DevOps Hands-on-Labs","url":"https://azuredevopslabs.com/labs/devopsserver/editorconfig/"},{"title":"Configuration Options","url":"https://ewsoftware.github.io/VSSpellChecker/html/fb81c214-0fe0-4d62-a172-d7928d5b91d5.htm"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about EditorConfig inheritance, override patterns, and specification:

## EditorConfig Inheritance and Override Patterns

### How Inheritance Works

As EditorConfig convention suggests, code styles defined in files named `.editorconfig` are applied in the directory of the current file and in all its parent directories until it reaches the root filepath or finds an EditorConfig file with `root=true`.(no content)To override some or all of the EditorConfig settings, add an `.editorconfig` file at the level of the file hierarchy you want those overridden settings to apply. The new EditorConfig file settings apply to files at the same level and files in any subdirectories.

### Override Behavior

If you want to override some, but not all of the settings, specify just those settings in the `.editorconfig` file. Only those properties that you explicitly list in the lower-level `.editorconfig` file are overridden. Other settings from any higher-level `.editorconfig` files continue to apply.(no content)EditorConfig files are read from top to bottom. If there are multiple properties with the same name, the most recently found property with the same name takes precedence.

### The `root` Property

`root` is a special property that should be specified at the top of the file outside of any sections. Set to `true` to stop `.editorconfig` files search on current file.(no content)By setting `root=true` in the `.editorconfig` file at a specific level, you can prevent the inheritance of settings from parent directories.

### The `unset` Value

For any property, a value of `unset` is to remove the effect of that property, even if it has been set before. For example, add `indent_size = unset` to undefine the `indent_size` property (and use editor default).

### File Pattern Sections

EditorConfig files are separated into sections based on patterns that match the files they apply to. For example, a filter like `[*.cs,*.js,code.ts]` would apply to any C# or JavaScript file, and files with the exact name `code.ts`. You may have as many sections as needed.(no content)File masks specified in `.editorconfig` files, for example `*Test.cs`, are also taken into account.

### Case Sensitivity

Currently all properties and values are case-insensitive. They are lowercased when parsed.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Configuration Files - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/configuration-files"},{"title":"Configuration Files (Deprecated) - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/configuration-files-deprecated"},{"title":"Evolving flat config with extends - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/blog/2025/03/flat-config-extends-define-config-global-ignores/"},{"title":"Support cascading eslint rules (nested config files)? · Issue #84 · ember-cli/broccoli-lint-eslint","url":"https://github.com/ember-cli/broccoli-lint-eslint/issues/84"},{"title":"eslintrc configuration cascading not being honored · Issue #97 · microsoft/vscode-eslint","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-eslint/issues/97"},{"title":"Allow to extend configuration objects instead of cascading environments config · Issue #1637 · eslint/eslint","url":"https://github.com/eslint/eslint/issues/1637"},{"title":"Configuration Files","url":"https://archive.eslint.org/docs/user-guide/configuring/configuration-files"},{"title":"Configuring ESLint","url":"https://denar90.github.io/eslint.github.io/docs/user-guide/configuring"},{"title":"Configuration Cascading and Hierarchy not work correctly · Issue #6602 · eslint/eslint","url":"https://github.com/eslint/eslint/issues/6602"},{"title":"Is there something like configuration cascading feature of eslint? · Issue #187 · standard/semistandard","url":"https://github.com/standard/semistandard/issues/187"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about ESLint configuration extends, inheritance, and cascading:

## ESLint Configuration: Extends, Inheritance & Cascading

### New Flat Config (eslint.config.js) - Current Standard

A configuration object uses extends to inherit all the traits of another configuration object or array (including rules, plugins, and language options) and can then modify all the options.(no content)The extends key is an array of values indicating which configurations to extend from. The elements of the extends array can be one of three values: a string that specifies the name of a configuration in a plugin. ESLint plugins can export predefined configurations.

**When to use Extends:**
- For reusing configurations - When you want to inherit and build upon existing configurations from plugins, shareable packages, or predefined configs.
- For plugin configuration - When applying recommended or specific configurations from ESLint plugins (e.g., example/recommended).
- For predefined configurations - When using ESLint's built-in configurations like js/recommended or js/all.

**When to use Cascading:**
- For file-specific rule - When you need different rules for different file patterns or directories.(no content)This page explains how to use flat config files. For the deprecated eslintrc format, see the deprecated documentation.

### Legacy .eslintrc Format (Deprecated)

A configuration file, once extended, can inherit all the traits of another configuration file (including rules, plugins, and language options) and modify all the options.

**Configuration Cascading:**
When using .eslintrc.* and package.json files for configuration, you can take advantage of configuration cascading. The configuration cascade works based on the location of the file being linted. If there is an .eslintrc file in the same directory as the file being linted, then that configuration takes precedence. ESLint then searches up the directory structure, merging any .eslintrc files it finds along the way until reaching either an .eslintrc file with root: true or the root directory.

**Stopping the cascade:**
Continue searching for .eslintrc.* and package.json files in ancestor directories up to and including the root directory or until a config with "root": true is found.

**Override precedence:**
Glob pattern overrides have higher precedence than the regular configuration in the same config file. Multiple overrides within the same config are applied in order. That is, the last override block in a config file always has the highest precedence.

There was also a March 2025 ESLint blog post titled "Evolving flat config with extends" that discusses these features, though the detailed content wasn't fully captured in the search results.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "Unix RC file history origin "run commands" dotfiles convention"

Links: [{"title":"What Does 'rc' Mean in Dot Files? Linux Configuration Files Explained — linuxvox.com","url":"https://linuxvox.com/blog/what-does-rc-mean-in-dot-files/"},{"title":"What Are Dotfiles? | Richie Codes","url":"https://www.richie.codes/blog/what-are-dotfiles/"},{"title":"Meaning of RC in a Dotfile - Today I Learned","url":"https://til.hashrocket.com/posts/zf2nzhqnsx-meaning-of-rc-in-a-dotfile"},{"title":"The Meaning of 'rc' in Dot Files - Javascript","url":"https://copyprogramming.com/howto/what-does-rc-mean-in-dot-files"},{"title":"What Is rc and What Are rc Files? | Baeldung on Linux","url":"https://www.baeldung.com/linux/rc-files"},{"title":"why we have rc word in config file names","url":"https://www.softpost.org/programming/why-we-have-rc-word-in-config-file-names"},{"title":"Guide to Unix/Files - Wikibooks, open books for an open world","url":"https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/Guide_to_Unix/Files"}]

Here's what I found about the history and origin of Unix RC files and dotfiles:

## Origin of "RC" in Unix Files

The 'rc' suffix is ubiquitous in dot files, but its origin is surprisingly debated. The most widely accepted explanation traces 'rc' to "Run Commands", a term from early computing history.

### The CTSS Connection

In the 1960s, MIT developed the Compatible Time-Sharing System (CTSS)—one of the first time-sharing operating systems. CTSS included a feature called "runcom" (short for "run commands"), which executed a script of commands stored in a file. Users could create RUNCOM files to automate tasks, and this concept later influenced Unix.(no content)The term is thought to have originated in 1965 from the runcom facility in the MIT Compatible Time-Sharing System (CTSS).

### How It Came to Unix

When Unix was developed in the 1970s, its creators (Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie) borrowed the "run commands" idea. The system's startup script was named /etc/rc (still used today in some Linux distros), short for "run commands." Over time, this convention trickled down to user-specific configuration files like .bashrc and .vimrc.(no content)"There existed a facility named 'runcom' that would execute a set of commands stored in a file. This file was commonly referred to as 'a runcom,' and the abbreviation 'rc' in Unix originated from this usage."

### Alternative Interpretation

The rc in .zshrc stands for "run commands" or "run control", depending on who you ask.(no content)The "rc" suffix originally stood for "run control," indicating that these files controlled how the shell or other programs would run or behave during startup.

## Dotfiles Convention

In UNIX, there's a convention whereby the names of files and folders which are meant to be hidden from view by default are prefixed with a dot. Unsurprisingly, these are called "dotfiles" or "dot folders". When they say "hidden", they mean hidden from the ls command.(no content)Dot files (or "hidden files") are configuration files in Unix-like systems whose names start with a dot (.). This naming convention hides them by default from standard ls commands (you'll need ls -a to list them). They live in your home directory (~) and control the behavior of applications, shells, and system tools.

### Common Examples

Over time, the "rc" naming convention has been adopted for various configuration files beyond shell scripts, such as .vimrc for configuring the Vim text editor or .bashrc for configuring the Bash shell environment.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Dotfiles - Shared Config for ZSH and Bash - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/michaelcurrin/dotfiles-shared-config-for-zsh-and-bash-4ff9"},{"title":"ZSH — dotfiles 1.0.0 documentation","url":"https://canux-dotfiles.readthedocs.io/en/latest/zsh.html"},{"title":"GitHub - htlin222/dotfiles: my dotfile on macOS, include neovim, zshrc, .etc","url":"https://github.com/htlin222/dotfiles"},{"title":"dotfiles/.zshrc at main · driesvints/dotfiles","url":"https://github.com/driesvints/dotfiles/blob/main/.zshrc"},{"title":"GitHub - thoughtbot/dotfiles: A set of vim, zsh, git, and tmux configuration files.","url":"https://github.com/thoughtbot/dotfiles"},{"title":"The Ultimate Guide to Mastering Dotfiles","url":"https://www.daytona.io/dotfiles/ultimate-guide-to-dotfiles"},{"title":"why do I need .zshrc? - Apple Community","url":"https://discussions.apple.com/thread/256027209"},{"title":"I Recently Switched to Zsh and Created a Dotfiles Install Script — Nick Janetakis","url":"https://nickjanetakis.com/blog/i-recently-switched-to-zsh-and-created-a-dotfiles-install-script"},{"title":"GitHub - mindkeep/dotfiles: unified zshrc/bashrc/kshrc/shrc scripts plus other generally useful dotfiles","url":"https://github.com/mindkeep/dotfiles"},{"title":"dotfiles/.zshrc at master · dariusgrassi/dotfiles","url":"https://github.com/dariusgrassi/dotfiles/blob/master/.zshrc"}]

Based on my search results, here's information about zshrc/bashrc source include mechanisms in dotfiles:

## Shared Configuration Between Zsh and Bash

When using ZSH as the default shell on macOS and Bash on Linux, you need to maintain both .zshrc and .bashrc files. Some code in those is specific to the OS, but most logic can go in a shared file. A common approach is to create a `.commonrc` file that gets loaded by both ZSH and Bash config files. If you make a change to the common config, it gets applied in both shells.

## Source Command Usage

The `source` command is the primary mechanism for including other files:

In your .bashrc, you would add `source ~/.commonrc` to load the shared configuration, followed by Bash-specific content.(no content)A common pattern for conditional sourcing checks if the file exists first: `[[ -f "/path/to/file.zsh" ]] && builtin source "/path/to/file.zsh"`

## Organized Configuration Directories

Additional zsh configuration can go under a `~/dotfiles-local/zsh/configs` directory. This has two special subdirectories: `pre` for files that must be loaded first, and `post` for files that must be loaded last.

## Unified Shell Scripts

The various `.*shrc`, `.*profile`, and `.*logout` files are shell-specific and primarily responsible for setting up the prompt for each shell. Each of these can source `.shrc.common` and `.shrc.local` files. The `.shrc.common` and `.shrc.local` files should be written in POSIX shell so that they are portable to other *sh implementations.

## Key Differences Between Shells

You should not simply use `mv` to change a .bashrc file to .zshrc. The two don't use the same syntax for everything, and if you do that you may have lines in .bashrc that will cause Zsh to experience a fatal error and crash.(no content)The .bashrc and .zshrc files configure your shell environment, setting aliases, functions, and other preferences.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

results:
Based on my search results, here's information about zshrc/bashrc source include mechanisms in dotfiles:

## Shared Configuration Between Zsh and Bash

When using ZSH as the default shell on macOS and Bash on Linux, you need to maintain both .zshrc and .bashrc files. Some code in those is specific to the OS, but most logic can go in a shared file. A common approach is to create a `.commonrc` file that gets loaded by both ZSH and Bash config files. If you make a change to the common config, it gets applied in both shells.

## Source Command Usage

The `source` command is the primary mechanism for including other files:

In your .bashrc, you would add `source ~/.commonrc` to load the shared configuration, followed by Bash-specific content.(no content)A common pattern for conditional sourcing checks if the file exists first: `[[ -f "/path/to/file.zsh" ]] && builtin source "/path/to/file.zsh"`

## Organized Configuration Directories

Additional zsh configuration can go under a `~/dotfiles-local/zsh/configs` directory. This has two special subdirectories: `pre` for files that must be loaded first, and `post` for files that must be loaded last.

## Unified Shell Scripts

The various `.*shrc`, `.*profile`, and `.*logout` files are shell-specific and primarily responsible for setting up the prompt for each shell. Each of these can source `.shrc.common` and `.shrc.local` files. The `.shrc.common` and `.shrc.local` files should be written in POSIX shell so that they are portable to other *sh implementations.

## Key Differences Between Shells

You should not simply use `mv` to change a .bashrc file to .zshrc. The two don't use the same syntax for everything, and if you do that you may have lines in .bashrc that will cause Zsh to experience a fatal error and crash.(no content)The .bashrc and .zshrc files configure your shell environment, setting aliases, functions, and other preferences.

Links: [{"title":"General-purpose dotfiles utilities - dotfiles.github.io","url":"https://dotfiles.github.io/utilities/"},{"title":"GitHub - thoughtbot/rcm: rc file (dotfile) management","url":"https://github.com/thoughtbot/rcm"},{"title":"GitHub does dotfiles - dotfiles.github.io","url":"https://dotfiles.github.io/"},{"title":"GitHub - webpro/awesome-dotfiles: A curated list of dotfiles resources.","url":"https://github.com/webpro/awesome-dotfiles"},{"title":"Yet Another Dotfiles Manager - yadm","url":"https://yadm.io/"},{"title":"Ubuntu Manpage: rcm — dotfile management","url":"https://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/focal/man7/rcm.7.html"},{"title":"Managing Dotfiles With Style With rcm","url":"https://distro.tube/guest-articles/managing-dotfiles-with-rcm.html"},{"title":"GitHub - Lissy93/dotfiles: 🧰 ~ • My Đotfiles","url":"https://github.com/Lissy93/dotfiles"},{"title":"Managing Your Dotfiles","url":"https://anishathalye.com/2014/08/03/managing-your-dotfiles"},{"title":"Ask HN: What do you use to manage dotfiles? | Hacker News","url":"https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=11070797"}]

Based on my search results, here's a comprehensive comparison of dotfile and RC configuration management systems:

## Top Dotfile Management Systems

### Most Popular by GitHub Stars:

1. **chezmoi** (17,271 stars) - Makes it easy to manage your dotfiles across multiple machines, securely. It's easy to install, quick to start with, and very powerful. You can install chezmoi and your dotfiles with a single command, without needing Python installed, or even git.

2. **Mackup** (15,039 stars) - An application settings and configuration manager that automatically finds the location of settings files for a set of known applications and syncs them with Dropbox, Git, or any other supported method.

3. **Home Manager** (9,118 stars) - A system built for managing NixOS user environments using the Nix package manager and the Nixpkgs libraries.

4. **Dotbot** (7,733 stars) - A lightweight standalone tool to bootstrap dotfiles, making it easy to have a "one click" installation/upgrade process for your dotfiles.

### Other Notable Tools:

- **Homeshick** (2,178 stars) - Like Homesick but written in bash.
- **dotdrop** (1,880 stars) - Save your dotfiles once, deploy them everywhere.
- **dotter** (1,820 stars) - A dotfile manager and templater written in Rust, with Windows/Linux/Mac support.

### RCM (thoughtbot):
RCM assumes that you have a separate dotfiles directory, or are interested in creating one. The programs provided are rcup(1), mkrc(1), rcdn(1), and lsrc(1), explained in the tutorial and configured using rcrc(5).(no content)The rcm suite was built as an abstraction over the shell, Ruby, Python, and make scripts people were writing and sharing. It is intended to run on any unix system and support the range from simple to complex dotfile directories.

### YADM (Yet Another Dotfiles Manager):


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Git - git-config Documentation","url":"https://git-scm.com/docs/git-config"},{"title":"Conditionally setting your gitconfig | utf9k","url":"https://utf9k.net/blog/conditional-gitconfig/"},{"title":"Today I Learned: .gitconfig's includeIf","url":"https://www.cloudbees.com/blog/today-i-learned-gitconfigs-includeif"},{"title":"Using includeIf to manage your git identities | by Gillis J. de Nijs | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@mrjink/using-includeif-to-manage-your-git-identities-bcc99447b04b"},{"title":"Git Conditional Includes","url":"https://www.edwardthomson.com/blog/git_conditional_includes"},{"title":"How to Use .gitconfig's includeIf","url":"https://dzone.com/articles/how-to-use-gitconfigs-includeif"},{"title":"Conditional Git Config","url":"https://www.leemeichin.com/posts/conditional-git-config.html"},{"title":"Modularizing your git config with conditional includes | Whisper of the Heartman","url":"https://blog.thomasheartman.com/posts/modularizing-your-git-config-with-conditional-includes/"},{"title":"Conditional includes in .gitconfig not supported · Issue #12041 · desktop/desktop","url":"https://github.com/desktop/desktop/issues/12041"},{"title":"📦 Multiple Git Configurations and Identities with Folder-Dependent Includes for GitLab, GitHub et al","url":"https://jdsalaro.com/tutorial/git-configuration-folder-dependent-conditional-includes/"}]

Here's what I found about Git's `include` and `includeIf` conditional configuration:

## Overview

Git has a wonderful directive called `[includeIf]`. Basically with this directive you can import an additional configuration file based on a condition being true.(no content)The `include` and `includeIf` sections work the same, except the `includeIf` has a condition that must be satisfied. They let you insert configuration from another file into your main one.

## How It Works

You can conditionally include a config file from another by setting an `includeIf.<condition>.path` variable to the name of the file to be included. The condition starts with a keyword followed by a colon and some data whose format and meaning depends on the keyword.

### The `gitdir` Keyword

The data that follows the keyword `gitdir` and a colon is used as a glob pattern. If the location of the .git directory matches the pattern, the include condition is met.(no content)The pattern can contain standard globbing wildcards and two additional ones, `**/` and `/**`, that can match multiple path components.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.
